arc: Arc 1 — The World After the Gates
title: The Stable Age
summary: Earth believes the hunter era is stable. Cheon’s hidden Chronicle-class authority is foreshadowed, the Ten Stars argue about Mael’s absence, and Mael tests his pathetic artificial status windows in abandoned countries before the Busan Banshee warning interrupts everything.
content: New Seoul looked peaceful because peace had become expensive.

Morning light slid across glass towers, electric buses, drone lanes, rooftop gardens, guild advertisements, and security barriers designed to look like decoration. People moved through crosswalks with coffee in one hand and phones in the other while giant screens above them sold the dream of awakening like it was a college program.
On one screen, a masked A-Rank hunter split a troll in half inside a dungeon corridor. The replay slowed right before the blade landed. Sparks burst. The crowd in the video screamed his name.
Below it, bright letters rolled across the billboard.
TEN YEARS SINCE THE FIRST AWAKENING.
The next advertisement replaced it before anyone could think too deeply about the word strong.
YOUR STATUS COULD CHANGE YOUR LIFE.
A boy in a school uniform stopped beside his mother and watched the hunter replay again. His eyes shone with the particular stupidity of children who had never smelled a dungeon.
His mother did not look up from her phone. ‘Just make sure you awaken as something safe. Your uncle awakened as a shield porter and still complains every day.’
The boy frowned. ‘Shield porter is still a hunter.’
‘Shield porter is how your uncle learned to sleep sitting up.’
Awakening had become normal enough for jokes.
Gates had become normal enough for traffic reports.
Hunters had become normal enough for advertisements.
Monsters had become normal enough that the public only cared when a gate broke near property values.
Humanity had survived the first decade of the hunter era, and survival had made people arrogant.
Every schoolchild knew the official timeline.

Humans awakened before the gates. Status windows appeared in front of random people across the world. Some gained strength, some gained senses, some gained healing, some gained useless tricks, and some gained powers governments immediately tried to classify.

Before monsters became the greatest threat, awakened humans did. Criminals, mercenaries, warlords, cults, private armies, and frightened governments tore into each other. The Awakened Wars were not clean wars. They were alley executions, coup attempts, forced recruitment, black markets, guild assassinations, and cities learning that one angry B-Rank could kill more people than a gang with rifles.

The first global treaties formed. The Hunter Association gained teeth. Guilds were recognized, licensed, taxed, bribed, watched, and used. Countries that could control awakened people became stable. Countries that could not became hunting grounds for everyone else.
F-Rank gates opened first. Then E. Then D. Then C. The world panicked for six months, then learned to monetize the apocalypse.
The first SSS Gate did not open in a superpower. It opened in Haiti, in a country the world thought it could afford to abandon.
Every official textbook mentioned that line differently.
Mael Pierre had never removed it.
By 2020, the stable age had begun.

World Gate Command existed. The Hunter Association had international authority. Major guilds had become half-corporations, half-armies, and half-religions if one counted how people screamed when their favorite S-Rank smiled on camera.
Ten hunters recognized not only for power, but for what the world believed they represented.
Cheon Ito Kabouke, First Star of Humanity.
Elias Stormwell, Lightning Sovereign of the American First Guild.
Mael Pierre, The Living Archive of the Haitian First Guild.
Nadine Saint-Fleur, Saint of Blood and Bone.
Publicly, they were humanity’s answer to extinction.
Privately, half of them disagreed on almost everything.
That morning, they were disagreeing about Mael again.
The secure conference room inside Korean First Guild did not look like a war room. Cheon disliked theatrical furniture. No round black table. No skull-shaped dungeon trophies. No dramatic lighting.
Just a long matte-white table, reinforced glass walls, old maps, newer screens, and a silence that made executives sit straighter.
Cheon Ito Kabouke stood at the head of the room with both hands behind his back.
People felt his sword even when the sword was not drawn.

Absolute Blade Authority made the air near him honest. Weak hunters felt their mana still. Strong hunters felt their instincts become careful. Executives who had survived assassination attempts lowered their voices without knowing why.
On the main screen, ten names glowed.
Victor Hale leaned back in his chair, metal fingers tapping against the table. He had replaced his left hand three times because he kept improving the design and calling it maintenance.
Victor said, ‘Let me guess. He is in another country with bad roads, worse internet, and a theory that should not work.’
Elias Stormwell snorted through the speaker. He was calling from an American First Guild aircraft somewhere above the Atlantic, lightning flickering behind him through the window.
Elias said, ‘That is generous. Last time, he missed a crisis meeting because he was trying to teach a village midwife how to read a fake status window.’
Yuna Baek sat with perfect posture, pale hair falling over one shoulder. ‘It was not fake. It was inferior.’
Victor pointed at her. ‘That is worse.’
Marcus Crowe’s voice came from a second screen. He sounded half asleep. ‘Is this the part where we all pretend we do not know why he is Rank Three?’
Elias’s smile sharpened. ‘I know why the public argues. I still want him present when the rest of us have to answer for his experiments.’
Elias lifted both hands. ‘I respect Mael. I also respect not giving prototype soul-adjacent system architecture to people in unstable regions and hoping the universe applauds.’
Han Seojun appeared from a shadowed corner that no one had seen him enter. ‘He is not hoping. He is measuring failure.’
Victor sighed. ‘Great. That makes it sound safe.’
He looked at Mael’s gray name on the screen.

Other people saw Mael Pierre as a contradiction. Rank Three, but not the strongest attacker. Haitian First Guild, but always moving through Ghana, Haiti, Nigeria, rural Brazil, coastal Indonesia, mountain villages in Nepal, places major guilds visited only after cameras arrived.

The Living Archive. A man who carried libraries in his head, built models in black-red mana, wrote emergency manuals for cities that could not afford elite guild contracts, and argued with world leaders like they were students who had failed to read the chapter.
To the public, Mael was a support genius.
To Cheon, Mael was what remained after heroes died.
Cheon said, ‘He is where he needs to be.’
Elias’s expression changed. Not anger. Frustration. ‘Cheon, the summit is in three days.’

Victor pulled up a side report. ‘Because according to his latest encrypted packet, he is still calling his project pathetic. Direct quote. Pathetic compared to the true System, but less pathetic than doing nothing.’
That made the room still in a different way.
Everyone knew Mael had been building something.

At first, people laughed at the phrase because it sounded impossible. Then they saw Mael make a dying C-Rank porter see his own blood pressure, mana flow, organ damage, stamina collapse, and recommended treatment steps in a floating black-red interface. After that, no one laughed. They only became afraid in more professional language.
Mael’s project had a long formal title because Mael named things like a man punishing future readers.
Everyone else called it Mael’s fake status.

The true System was clean, absolute, universal, and cruel. It touched the soul, defined rank, assigned skills, recognized classes, measured achievements, delivered quests, enforced dungeon rules, and recorded growth across reality itself.
Mael’s fake status was a shadow drawn with burnt fingers.
It could show a person where power might move if their body survived the attempt.
It could sometimes, under carefully controlled conditions, push a dormant awakened candidate toward a weak spark.
Mael had recorded the death personally and refused to remove it from the report.
The major guilds hated the project.
Because one day it might not.

If Mael succeeded, awakening would no longer belong entirely to luck, bloodline, guild scouting, military screening, and countries rich enough to build hunter academies. Poor countries might force their own awakenings. Villages might grow their own defenders. Children born outside guild zones might gain something better than waiting for monsters to arrive first.

Victor scrolled the report. ‘He is currently in northern Ghana. Before that, Cap-Haitien. Before that, a refugee camp outside Manaus. Before that, Port-au-Prince again. He is planting test windows through volunteer candidates in places with low awakened density and high gate casualty rates.’

Victor’s humor vanished. ‘Of forty-three candidates, twenty-nine rejected the interface entirely. Seven developed temporary mana fever. Four gained sensory improvements. Two achieved unstable F-Rank awakening. One awakened a utility skill for wound clotting. One died from prior organ weakness during forced circulation.’
Cheon’s eyes remained on the gray name.
Mael would carry that death forever.
That was why Cheon trusted him more than the people who wanted clean numbers.
People who forgot the dead became efficient monsters.
People who remembered every dead person could still become monsters, but at least they knew the price.
On the screen, Victor opened another message from Mael.
TRUE SYSTEM COMPARISON: CHILD SCRATCHING ON COSMIC GLASS.
PRACTICAL VALUE: LOCAL TRAINING, TRIAGE, BASIC AWAKENING PRESSURE, CIVILIAN POWER LITERACY.
STRATEGIC VALUE: THIRD-WORLD AWAKENING INDEPENDENCE IF STABILIZED.
REQUEST: DO NOT LET ELIAS NAME ANYTHING.
Han said, ‘Because you name things like a lightning sponsor.’
Marcus nodded on screen. ‘Stormborn Freedom Interface.’
Victor snapped his fingers. ‘Thunder Democracy Window.’
Nadine covered her mouth, but her shoulders moved once.
Elias looked betrayed. ‘All of you are children.’
Then his gaze shifted to the sealed file waiting beneath the reports.
It did not have a normal classification.
A few had powers that looked less like abilities and more like laws wearing human bodies.
The System called those powers by many names depending on translation, rank, and how much it wanted the user to understand.
One term appeared in old forbidden records.
Not chronic like sickness, though Victor once joked that the name fit because everyone infected with such power ruined history.
Chronicle-class powers did not merely kill monsters or heal wounds.
They recorded, claimed, named, inherited, conquered, or rewrote the meaning of worlds.
Most people believed Chronicle-class powers were myths invented by guild theorists who had spent too long reading dungeon inscriptions.
He had known since the day he awakened.
His first status window had shown a line that vanished after three seconds and never returned in public scans.
CONDITION: WORLD-LEVEL CLAIM, WORLD-LEVEL DEFENSE, OR TEN RECOGNIZED STARS.
He had not understood all of it then.
He understood enough to never speak of it casually.

Absolute Blade Authority became his public legend. The sword everyone saw. The clean white-blue slash that cut armor, curses, regeneration, spatial distortion, dungeon cores, authority fields, and fear.
But the hidden power behind him was not a sword.
It was a crown he refused to wear.
A power that whispered of stars, worlds, names, claim, and conquest.
A power that could, if the conditions were satisfied, make a being capable of standing above planets rather than merely defending them.
Cheon had spent years pretending not to hear that whisper.
Because Earth did not need another conqueror.
But some instinct deeper than strategy had guided him.
The condition line had stopped looking like coincidence years ago.
He did not know what he intended to say.
Maybe only to warn them that some powers were not meant to be used on Earth unless Earth itself became the battlefield.
Maybe only to tell them that if something ever came from beyond the System’s normal language, they were not to trust rankings.
Cheon looked at the Ten Stars’ names.
The conference room door opened without permission.
Jisoo Han, Korean First Guild’s emergency operations director, stepped in with a tablet in both hands and no color in her face.
That alone made every Star straighten.
Jisoo swallowed. ‘Busan Deep Nest has mutated.’
Jisoo sent the file to the main screen.
The image showed a drowned city underground.
Buildings leaned upside down. Rain fell upward. Roads curved into flooded ceilings. Streetlights glowed beneath black water. Human-shaped shadows stood in windows that should not exist inside a gate.
Three junior executives began crying before the sound was muted.
Nadine stood so fast her chair slid back.

Jisoo nodded. ‘Confirmed. But not normal. The song is affecting civilians outside the gate perimeter. We have thirty-seven missing already. They walked toward the exclusion zone without memory of leaving home.’
‘SS at minimum. Rising. The Association’s predictive model says if the queen completes the nest, Busan becomes a death choir within six hours.’
Han stepped fully out of the shadow.
Aiko’s voice came through another screen, small but steady. ‘Oppa?’
Cheon looked once more at the file he had almost opened.
Today humanity needed the sword it already understood.
Jisoo answered immediately. ‘Ten kilometers ordered. Twenty recommended. Civilian compliance failing because of song influence.’
‘Seal media access. Send counter-frequency support. Put Korean First on full emergency status. Notify World Gate Command and the Association that I am entering personally.’
Elias leaned toward the screen. ‘You want backup?’
Cheon’s gaze remained on the drowned city image.
‘If multiple Stars enter and the song adapts, casualties increase. This is a single-blade problem.’
The room accepted that because Cheon had never needed arrogance to be terrifying.
As he turned to leave, Victor called out, ‘What about Mael?’
Victor’s tone was less mocking now. ‘If this thing is affecting civilians through sound and memory, his models might help.’
‘Send him the data,’ Cheon said. ‘Do not request his return.’
‘Because he is trying to give power to people the world keeps abandoning. Busan has me.’
He left before anyone could answer.

Outside, Korean First Guild moved like a machine waking from sleep. Elevators locked. Armories opened. Sirens started low, then rose. Hunters ran without shouting. Operations screens shifted from summit preparation to disaster command.
Cheon walked through it all calmly.
Jisoo followed half a step behind.
She said quietly, ‘Guildmaster, what were you going to say in the meeting?’
For one moment, the hidden file seemed to burn behind his eyes.
A power that could make constellations.
A power that spoke of conquest.
A power he had spent years refusing to become.
Then the Busan gate alarm screamed across the building.
Jisoo understood the conversation was over.

Far away, in a clinic built from concrete blocks and donated solar panels in a country most major guilds only mentioned during charity speeches, Mael Pierre watched a young woman stare at a black-red window only she could see.
Mael stood in front of her, exhausted eyes reflecting black-red light.
He said in Haitian Creole first, then English for the doctor recording beside him, ‘Do not chase it. Let it touch you. If it hurts behind the eyes, say so. If your chest tightens, stop. No pride.’
Her village had lost eleven people to a D-Rank gate three months earlier because the nearest licensed hunter team arrived two days late and charged travel costs before clearing the nest.
She had volunteered after Mael explained the risk.
She asked him if doing nothing was safer.
So she sat in the chair.
She stared at the pathetic imitation of the System.
And for three seconds, a spark moved in her blood.
Mael looked at the girl’s trembling hands.
The girl laughed once, then cried.
Outside the clinic, children pressed against the window bars, trying to see whether someone poor could become something more than prey.
But the black-red window around his hand sharpened.
WARNING: IF QUEEN LEARNS INDIVIDUAL GRIEF PATTERNS, DO NOT LET CIVILIANS HEAR THEIR DEAD.
Then he looked back at the young woman whose weak new skill might one day save one bleeding child in one forgotten village.
The world would call that small.
Mael had the abandoned edges of the world.
And somewhere beyond both of them, history had already begun moving toward a door no ranking system would understand.
